content.full_text:
FEB-15-1990 13:17 FROM TO P.01

CITY OF PALOS VERDES ESTATES
340 PALOS VERDES DRIVE WEST
P.O. BOX 1086
PALOS VERDES ESTATES, CA 90274-0283

Telephone Number: (213) 378-0283
Facsimile Number: (213) 378-7820


TELECOPY COVER PAGE

From: Barbara Culver City Clerk

To: John Larson

Jurisdiction/Company: EPPG

Title of Document: Letter to John Larson

# of Pages (including transmittal page) 3

Date and Time Sent: 2-15-90 12 Noon

NOTES:


IF YOU HAVE ANY DIFFICULTY RECEIVING THIS
TRANSMISSION, PLEASE CALL BACK AS SOON AS POSSIBLE.

THANK YOU.

PAGE 001

FEB-15-1990 13:17 FROM

CITY OF PALOS VERDES ESTATES

(213) 378-0383

CITY HALL
POST OFFICE BOX 1086
PALOS VERDES ESTATES
CALIFORNIA 90274-0283

February 14, 1990

Mr. John H. Larson, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
Room 300
428 J Street Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Larson:

As City Clerk of the City of Palos Verdes Estates I have recently been in touch with the commission staff regarding one of the regulations referring to mailings and the prohibition against printing names of elected officials in the City Newsletter except in the masthead.

The City of Palos Verdes Estates is having their General Municipal election in April, 1990. I am an appointed City Clerk and I write the City Newsletter.

The City of Palos Verdes Estates has scrupulously adhered to the mass mailings prohibition since the passage of Proposition 73. The mass mailing of the City publishes a quarterly newsletter specifically to inform our residents regarding city issues and events. The edition which precedes the General Municipal Election by one week reminds the residents to vote, lists polling places, election time, and lists candidates to vote, lists polling places, election time, and lists the items on the local ballot such as ballot measures and candidates. This year we have six candidates on the ballot, one of whom, is an incumbent who is currently the Mayor.

I was advised by the commission staff that I could not list the incumbent by name. I was advised I could list the names of the five non-incumbent candidates but only list "Mayor" without a name attached to it. I protested this advice as being grossly unfair to the incumbent. At this time I suggested the City be allowed to reprint the sample ballot page listing the candidates' names since the City Clerk has previously had this printed and mailed to the voters and it is not reviewed by the City Council.

340 PALOS VERDES DRIVE WEST PALOS VERDES ESTATES, CALIFORNIA 90274-1299

FEB-15-1990 12:12 PAGE.002

FEB-15-1990 13:18 FROM                          TO                              P.03


     Today I was advised again that the Commission staff recommended
against this for two reasons:

     1) It would approve an exception which might precipitate more
exceptions.
     2) Our newsletter is reviewed by the Council prior to its mailing.

     I offered the possibility of having the City Newsletter reviewed
by the chairman for Council members, not up for election. I was
advised that this suggestion was also unacceptable.

     These two examples point to the need to re-evaluate this policy.
It is my considered opinion that this type of ruling was not the
intent of the voters who supported Proposition 73. The voters have
a right to know and be informed as to the facts of who is running
for office and who is not.

     I respectfully request that you ask the Commission to re-evaluate
this rule that prevents an incumbent's name being part of a
bonafide list of candidates in the City's newsletter.

     If the Commission makes a decision by March 7 please notify me so
that I can include either a sample ballot replica showing the
ballot measure and all candidates names or a description of the
measure with a sample list of candidates' names in the newsletter.

     Thank you for your consideration.


     Sincerely yours,

     [signature]
     Barbara J. Gulver
     City Clerk

     cc: Commission members
          City Council
          Council Candidates

The City of Palos Verdes Estates is having their General Municipal election in April, 1990. I am an appointed City Clerk and I write the City Newsletter. The City of Palos Verdes Estates has scrupulously adhered to the mass mailings prohibition since the passage of Proposition 73. The City publishes a quarterly newsletter specifically to inform our residents regarding city issues and events. The edition which precedes the General Municipal Election by one week reminds the residents to vote, lists polling places, election time, and lists the items on the local ballot such as ballot measures and candidates. This year we have six candidates on the ballot, one of whom, is an incumbent who is currently the Mayor. I was advised by the commission staff that I could not list the incumbent by name. I was advised I could list the names of the five non-incumbent candidates but only list "Mayor" without a name attached to it. I protested this advice as being grossly unfair to the incumbent. At this time I suggested the City be allowed to reprint the sample ballot page listing the candidates' names since the City Clerk has previously had this printed and mailed to the voters and it is not reviewed by the City Council. Today I was advised again that the Commission staff recommended against this for two reasons: 1) It would approve an exception which might precipitate more exceptions. 2) Our newsletter is reviewed by the Council prior to its mailing. I offered the possibility of having the City Newsletter reviewed by the remaining four Councilmembers, not up for election. I was advised that this suggestion was also unacceptable.
